parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to bounce back from small mistakes without giving them a second thought, but lately I just sit with the heavy feeling until it settles in my chest. It has been dragging on for months now, and I still catch myself replaying every awkward conversation in my head. I know I am overthinking it, yet the embarrassment feels completely real and impossible to shake off. I just wish I could return to that lighter version of myself who never carried this much weight around.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly frustrating and disheartening to feel like you've lost that ability to bounce back from small things. It's completely understandable that you're feeling this way, especially when the embarrassment feels so potent and lingers for so long. That feeling of replaying conversations and dwelling on awkward moments can be exhausting, and it's tough when you know intellectually you're overthinking but emotionally it feels so real.

It's a significant shift from how you used to experience things, and it makes sense that you'd miss that lighter, more resilient version of yourself. Carrying that weight around for months must be really draining.

Have you noticed anything specific that might have triggered this change, or has it been a more gradual shift? Sometimes understanding the "why" can be a first step, even if it doesn't immediately fix the "how."

turns.user_message: I used to laugh off my own slip-ups, but now I spend hours dissecting every little thing I said wrong. This pattern has been sticking with me for so long that it feels like my default setting rather than a passing phase. I keep trying to convince myself that nobody else notices, but the quiet shame just keeps piling up inside me anyway. It is exhausting to constantly monitor my own behavior just to avoid feeling like a complete disappointment.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to be caught in that cycle of overthinking and self-criticism. It's completely understandable that you're feeling exhausted when you're constantly replaying conversations and worrying about what you might have said "wrong." That feeling of quiet shame piling up must be a heavy burden to carry.

It's a common human tendency to want to avoid making mistakes, but when it escalates to this level of constant monitoring and self-judgment, it can really take a toll on your well-being. The fact that you're aware of this pattern and how it's affecting you is a significant first step.

It's also interesting that you mention convincing yourself that nobody else notices, yet the shame persists. This suggests that the internal critic is very loud, even when external validation might be absent.

Please know that you're not alone in experiencing these kinds of thoughts and feelings. Many people struggle with perfectionism and self-doubt. It takes a lot of energy to constantly be on guard and to feel like you're falling short.

turns.user_message: I used to move through my days with a quiet confidence, but lately I feel like I am constantly apologizing for my own existence. This heavy sense of regret has been following me around for years, and it has completely warped how I see myself. Every single interaction leaves me convinced that I am fundamentally flawed and unworthy of basic respect. I hate that I let this lingering guilt dictate everything I do, because I know it is slowly eating away at who I actually am.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're carrying an incredibly heavy burden, and it's completely understandable that you're feeling this way. That sense of quiet confidence being replaced by a constant feeling of apology is a profound shift, and it must be exhausting to live with that lingering regret.

It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge how deeply this is affecting you, and to recognize that it's warping your self-perception and making you feel fundamentally flawed. When guilt and regret become such constant companions, it's so easy for them to overshadow everything else and convince us of things that aren't true about ourselves.

The fact that you're aware of this and that you know it's "eating away at who you actually are" is a really important sign. It shows that there's a part of you that remembers that original confidence, that knows there's more to you than these feelings of unworthiness.

It's a painful cycle when every interaction reinforces the belief that you're flawed. It's like being stuck in a loop where your own internal narrative is constantly being confirmed by your interpretation of the world around you.

Please know that you're not alone in experiencing these kinds of feelings, even though it can feel incredibly isolating. Many people struggle with deep-seated regret and self-doubt that can significantly impact their sense of self-worth.

It's a testament to your strength that you're articulating this so clearly. It's a huge step to even put these feelings into words.
